reports.title: Iran's Hormuz Gambit: Trade the Blockade, Keep the Bomb
reports.subtitle: Tehran is offering to reopen the world's most important oil chokepoint in exchange for Washington dropping its nuclear demands. Trump is saying no. Both sides are right to distrust the other.

reports.title: SCOTUS Guts the Voting Rights Act, and the Ruling's Logic Goes Further Than Louisiana
reports.subtitle: The court's 6-3 decision in Louisiana v. Callais makes it nearly impossible to draw a majority-minority district without simultaneously making it unconstitutional. That paradox was the point.

reports.title: SCOTUS Signals It Will Let Trump Deport a Million People Without Court Review
reports.subtitle: The conservative majority appears ready to strip federal courts of jurisdiction over TPS cancellations, turning humanitarian protection into a purely executive decision with no check.

reports.title: China's New Trade Law Lets It Seize Foreign Assets. Trump Visits in Two Weeks.
reports.subtitle: Beijing quietly amended its Foreign Trade Law to authorize punishment of companies that reduce their business with China, just as US firms are quietly doing exactly that. The timing is not coincidental.

reports.title: Judge Calls Harvard Antisemitism Claims a Smokescreen. The Administration Just Revealed Its Real Strategy.
reports.subtitle: A federal judge ruled Trump illegally froze $2.6 billion in Harvard grants, calling it ideological punishment disguised as civil rights enforcement. The administration's response was to move the case to a different court.

reports.title: Musk Sues OpenAI for Stealing a Charity While Stealing Its Models
reports.subtitle: Under oath, Elon Musk admitted xAI distilled OpenAI technology to train Grok. He is also suing OpenAI for abandoning its mission. Both things are true, which is the problem.

reports.title: Putin's Victory Day Ceasefire Does Not Require Ukraine's Agreement. That Is the Point.
reports.subtitle: The Kremlin announced Russia will halt fighting on May 9 whether or not Kyiv agrees. Zelensky called it theater. Both men are right, and neither can afford to say what the gesture actually is.

reports.title: The US Economy Grew 2% While Consumers Ran Out of Cushion
reports.subtitle: GDP beat the shutdown slump. Business investment in AI is surging. Inflation hit a three-year high. Real wages are flat. The Fed is frozen. These are not contradictions. They describe the same economy from different floors.

reports.title: The SCOTUS Ruling Unlocked Maximum Gerrymandering. Republicans Are Running the Clock.
reports.subtitle: Within 48 hours of the Callais decision, Louisiana suspended its primaries, Florida passed a new map adding four Republican seats, and Trump called on Tennessee and other states to redraw their districts.

reports.title: Instagram Removed 7% of Reported Extremist Content. Meta Says That Is a Trade-Off.
reports.subtitle: A new ADL study found white supremacist networks, terror-group supporters, and Nazi merchandise vendors thriving on Instagram after Meta gutted its moderation team. Zuckerberg called it a trade-off.

reports.title: Anthropic Said No to the Pentagon. Google Said Yes. Now the White House Wants Anthropic Back.
reports.subtitle: The DoD branded Anthropic a supply-chain risk for refusing unrestricted military AI access. Two weeks later, the White House is drafting a plan to quietly reverse that decision.

reports.title: Nebraska Just Started Kicking People Off Medicaid for Not Working. The Federal Government Won't Track What Happens Next.
reports.subtitle: The biggest change to Medicaid since the ACA is rolling out state by state, with no federal reporting requirement, definitions that aren't finalized, and a January 2027 deadline that 43 states have to hit without knowing the rules.

reports.title: CISA Lost a Third of Its Staff. Cyber Partnerships Are at a Standstill. The Midterms Are Six Months Away.
reports.subtitle: The agency responsible for protecting US critical infrastructure and election systems has been hollowed out just as the 2026 midterms approach, and the House just passed a funding bill that cuts it further.

reports.title: Europe Just Had Its Biggest Military Spending Surge Since 1953. It Is Not Enough and Everyone Knows It.
reports.subtitle: European NATO members grew defense spending 14% in 2025, the steepest climb since the Korean War era. The NATO target just moved to 5% of GDP. The gap between current spending and what the war calculus demands is still enormous.

reports.title: Volkswagen Says Its Future Is at Risk. It Is Now Considering Letting Chinese Rivals Build Cars in Its Own European Factories.
reports.subtitle: VW reported a 14% profit collapse in Q1 2026 while warning cuts so far are not enough. The proposed solution is to share underutilized European plants with BYD and other Chinese brands that are competing with it everywhere.

reports.title: Trump Declared the Iran War 'Terminated.' US Forces Are Still There.
reports.subtitle: The administration invoked a legal fiction to bypass a 53-year-old law, and Congress let it happen. The precedent is more dangerous than the war itself.

reports.title: EU Lawmakers Tried to Delay Their AI Law. They Couldn't Agree How. The Deadline Is Still August 2.
reports.subtitle: The trilogue that was supposed to give AI companies until 2027 to comply collapsed over machinery regulations. Every high-risk AI system in Europe now has 94 days to meet rules most deployers haven't read.

reports.title: The Government Has to Return $166 Billion in Illegal Tariffs. Its Portal Is Rejecting 15% of Claims.
reports.subtitle: After the Supreme Court struck down Trump's IEEPA tariffs, the administration built a refund system and then quietly set it to deny one in seven valid claims. The first checks go out May 11.

reports.title: Congress Extended Warrantless Surveillance for 45 More Days. It Has Done This Twice This Month.
reports.subtitle: FISA Section 702 lets the government collect Americans' communications without a warrant when they talk to foreigners. A bipartisan coalition came within striking distance of real reform. Congress chose the extension instead.

reports.title: Iran Has Been Offline for 90 Days. The IRGC Wants to Keep It That Way. The Government Doesn't.
reports.subtitle: Iran's internet shutdown is now the longest national blackout in modern history. It is also tearing open a power struggle between the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and the civilian government that both sides are trying to hide.
